checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain any fabricated paragraphs or sentences. All text content in the JSON (the list items and the table cells) corresponds directly to text found in the source document. The metadata title and image alt text are within the bounds of acceptable AI-generated additions.
The source document contains no phone numbers or email addresses. The project names and department names in the output footer match the source exactly. There are no fabricated URLs or addresses.
The source is a single-page document. The output represents the page through a combination of text and image elements. No entire section or page is missing.

The footer table in the source has two rows of information (organized as a grid). The JSON output represents this correctly as a table with two rows, preserving all data fields (Date, Project, Created By, Department, Description).

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output fails to include a significant amount of text content found in the source document. Specifically, the 'VLANs & Subnets' section (containing VLAN IDs like '210' and '410') and numerous descriptive labels within the network diagram (e.g., 'State Network', 'Internet Access', 'Agency Networks', 'OOB Management', 'Data Center', 'Enterprise AD', 'NCID', etc.) are completely absent from the JSON's text elements. Although the diagram is captured as an image, these labels represent substantial textual information that accounts for more than 25% of the total text content on the page.
checks.b_evidence_source: VLANs & Subnets section: 'Employee – 210, agency', 'NCGovRoam – 410, guest', 'NCGov Guest – 410, guest'. Diagram labels: 'State Network', 'Internet Access', 'Agency Networks', 'Data Center', 'Enterprise AD', 'NCID', 'Policy Servers (EDC & WDC)'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The text content for these sections is entirely missing from the JSON elements; it is not present in the list or the table.
checks.b_evidence_location: Page 1, diagram area and VLAN section below the diagram.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text that was captured (the top list and the bottom table) is accurately transcribed with only minor punctuation/spacing differences which are acceptable.

checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain duplicated text blocks. Each element in the source, such as the SSID descriptions and the footer table entries, appears only once in the JSON structure.

The source document contains visual section identifiers within the diagram (e.g., 'OOB Management', 'Data Center', 'VLANs & Subnets'), which are not captured as headings in the output. However, the output contains no heading tags (H1-H6) at all, meaning there is no hierarchy to be 'broken' in the technical sense of skipping levels.

The table at the bottom of the document has a corrupted structure. In the source, the first row contains three distinct columns. The second row contains two main labels. In the output JSON, the first row correctly has three cells, but the second row has only two cells without any 'colspan' attributes to reconcile the grid. This results in a broken table grid where columns do not align vertically.
checks.b_evidence_source: The footer table has a 3-column layout (Date, Project, Created By) followed by a row with 2 main entries (Department, Description).
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'table' element (reading_order: 2) has a first row with 3 cells and a second row with only 2 cells, failing to maintain the 3-column structure.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order of the document is significantly altered. The source document's logical flow is: SSID descriptions (top) -> Diagram (middle) -> Table (bottom). The output JSON places the footer table (Element 2) before the diagram content (represented as a series of images starting at Element 3).
checks.b_evidence_source: The table containing 'Date: April 1 2025' is located at the bottom of the page, below the diagram.
checks.b_evidence_output: The table (reading_order: 2) is placed before the images that constitute the diagram (reading_order: 3+).

checks.a_analysis: The three paragraphs at the top of the page describing SSIDs are converted into an unordered list. This is an acceptable structural normalization for screen readers and preserves the meaning and relationship of the items.
There are no links or URLs present in either the source document or the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The source document is a single-page network diagram with text descriptions, a visual flowchart/topology with icons and arrows, a metadata table, and a state seal. In the HTML output, the visual organization is completely destroyed. The diagram components (clouds, servers, seal) have been extracted as individual image fragments and stacked vertically, losing all spatial relationships, connection lines, and flow. A user cannot understand the network topology from the converted version as the visual organization has been collapsed into a linear list of disjointed graphics.
checks.b_evidence_source: A coherent network diagram with icons for 'Guests', 'State Employees', 'Agency Networks', and 'Data Center' connected by various labeled arrows.
checks.b_evidence_output: A series of fragmented, vertically stacked image icons (green/red clouds, server icons, state seal) without any of the original connecting arrows or logical layout.
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the entire rendered HTML content following the initial text and table.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF contains a metadata table at the bottom of the page. The HTML output renders this as a standard HTML table. While the styling is simplified, the rows and columns are clearly defined, and the data is readable and organized correctly.

The primary meaningful content of the source PDF is the network diagram image. In the HTML output, this diagram is not rendered as a single coherent image. Instead, it has been sliced into numerous small fragments. Crucially, the connecting arrows and lines that convey the 'logic' of the diagram are missing or broken, appearing only as unidentifiable slivers of color between larger fragments. The meaningful diagram is effectively broken and unusable.
checks.b_evidence_source: A complex network diagram containing icons, labels, and connecting arrows showing data flow.
checks.b_evidence_output: Dozens of small, disconnected image fragments and cropped icons that do not represent the whole diagram and omit the connecting line work.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main body of the HTML output.

checks.a_analysis: There are no instances of body text, tables, or entire sections being duplicated in the HTML output. The textual content from the diagram (SSID descriptions and VLAN info) appears only once.
